arguments.task: Open official Adobe Commerce developer documentation and related official Adobe Commerce pages. Determine: one-line product description, all documented API auth methods, whether API access is self-serve free/trial/paid/admin/partner gated, API surface type and breadth, whether an official MCP server exists, rate limit notes, and any notable buildability signals. Prefer canonical first-party docs pages that load in browser and explicitly support these fields, including developer docs, auth docs, API docs, pricing or product pages. Return the exact URLs read and concise evidence-backed findings only.

input.thought: Start a focused browser run on official Adobe Commerce sources to gather evidence-backed documentation details.

raw_result.description: Adobe Commerce is Adobe’s ecommerce platform with extensible REST and GraphQL APIs for building and managing storefront and commerce operations.
raw_result.auth_notes: Official Adobe Commerce Web API docs describe token-based authentication and OAuth 1.0a for Commerce web APIs, and also describe Adobe Identity Management Services (IMS) authentication for Adobe Commerce as a Cloud Service/SaaS APIs. OAuth 1.0a is mapped to oauth2 only because the schema does not allow oauth1; IMS is mapped to other.
raw_result.gating_reason: Public Adobe Commerce developer documentation for web APIs is openly accessible without partner-only or contact-sales gating evidence on the docs reviewed.

api_surface.breadth: Official docs expose both REST and GraphQL surfaces across broad commerce capabilities, with dedicated Web API documentation and GraphQL coverage for storefront and backend commerce use cases.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Adobe Commerce MCP server was verified from the gathered first-party pages.

raw_result.blocker: Auth documentation mixes OAuth 1.0a, bearer tokens, and IMS depending on product area, so building a general-purpose toolkit requires narrowing scope to the documented Commerce web APIs or handling multiple auth schemes.
raw_result.rate_limit_notes: Official docs reviewed indicate rate limiting support focused on payment information APIs, configurable with Redis-backed settings; no general platform-wide numeric API rate limits were verified from the gathered pages.
raw_result.notable_signals: Strong first-party developer portal; dedicated web API documentation; documented REST and GraphQL support; explicit rate-limiting documentation; multiple auth paths covering traditional Commerce APIs and SaaS/Cloud Service APIs.
raw_result.human_review_reason: Two structured fields are not fully supported by the gathered evidence alone: auth_methods requires schema-forced approximation because the docs indicate OAuth 1.0a rather than OAuth 2.0, and mcp_exists was not directly verified from a first-party page reviewed.
